original_abstract: This article provides a critical examination of the European Union’s practice of incorporating “essential elements” clauses into its trade agreements. This policy serves as the primary mechanism for operationalizing the trade-human rights nexus, fulfilling the EU’s constitutional mandate to promote its core values through external relations. Following an analysis of the legal anatomy of these clauses, the article investigates three primary areas of scholarly and political critique: the inconsistent inclusion of these clauses across different trade partners, concerns regarding their specific drafting and material scope, and the persistent challenges surrounding their monitoring and enforcement. The study concludes by assessing the overall effectiveness and legitimacy of this policy, ultimately weighing its impact on the credibility of the EU’s normative agenda in the global arena.
humanized_abstract: Abuses, distortions and challenges: the Erosion of the Protection of Human and Labour Rights through Essential Elements in EU Free Trade Agreements Part III: the legitimacy of EU essential elements practice This third article carries the analysis of the EU’s “essential elements” practice as laid out in Part I and critiqued in Part II. After addressing the legal composition of “essential elements”, the second section turns to address the criticisms made to this practice at the scholarly and at the political levels, specifically in relation to three of them: the uneven position of essential elements in relation to the varying number of FTAs which are being negotiated by the EU and, therefore, the diverging and highly complex position of the relevant standards in these different contexts; 5.3.1 their specific formulation and, therefore, to their uncertain material scope of application in international economic life; 5.3.2 the means designed for the control of their application by those to whom they are directly applicable (States and, particularly, enterprises); 5.3.3 the legitimate questions related to their application and to the practical impacts on the human and labour rights it is designed to protect, a point specifically examined in the second article, focusing in particular on the example of labour rights. In light of all these challenges, this part draws conclusions regarding the legitimacy of such an important EU practice as is the application of the “essential elements clause” for the promotion of European human and labour rights within international trade through the effective implementation of WTO agreements.
